title: Optimization of two-photon excitation by indistinguishable photons in a three-level atom
authors: Masood Valipour, Gniewomir Sarbicki, Karolina Słowik, Anita Dąbrowska
abstract: We investigate the excitation of a three-level ladder-type atom by a unidirectional field with a pair of indistinguishable photons. Starting from an analytical expression for the two-photon absorption probability, we determine the two-photon state that maximizes the population of the upper atomic state at a chosen time and show that, in the limit of an infinitely long pulse, perfect excitation is possible. The optimal state is identified as the time-reversed counterpart of the two-photon state emitted in spontaneous cascade decay. We then compare this ideal excitation strategy with experimentally accessible families of states, including symmetrized Gaussian product states, temporally correlated Gaussian states, and coherent pulses. We analyze how the optimal excitation conditions depend on the ratio of atomic decay rates and on the separation of the atomic transition frequencies. For indistinguishable photons described by Gaussian pulses, quantum interference may shift the maxima of the marginal spectral distribution away from the atomic resonances and qualitatively modify the optimal excitation strategy. Our results clarify the role of indistinguishability and correlations in two-photon absorption and provide guidance for designing realistic excitation schemes in quantum-optical light-matter interfaces .
summary: This paper investigates how to maximize the excitation of a three-level atom using pairs of indistinguishable photons. The authors find that the most efficient excitation occurs when the input light is the time-reversed version of the atom's own spontaneous decay process. By comparing this ideal state to practical Gaussian and coherent pulses, they demonstrate how quantum interference and photon correlations can be tuned to optimize light-matter interactions in quantum-optical interfaces.
main_problem: Optimizing the two-photon excitation probability of a three-level ladder-type atom using indistinguishable photons and comparing this with experimentally accessible Gaussian and coherent states.
main_result: The authors identify an optimal two-photon state, which is the time-reversed counterpart of spontaneous cascade decay, capable of achieving perfect excitation in the limit of an infinitely long pulse. They also show that quantum interference in Gaussian pulses can shift spectral maxima away from atomic resonances.
method: The study employs the Wigner-Weisskopf approximation, input-output formalism, and a quantum-trajectory/stochastic approach to solve the hierarchy of coupled differential equations for the atomic density matrix.
model_or_system: A three-level atom in a ladder configuration (|g>, |e>, |f>) interacting with a unidirectional, continuous-mode two-photon field. The dynamics are governed by a master equation within the Wigner-Weisskopf approximation.
key_observables: Population of the upper atomic state P_f(t), marginal probability densities in time and frequency domains, and average residence time in the final state.
important_parameters: Atomic decay rates (Gamma_e, Gamma_f), atomic transition frequency difference (delta_a), photon pulse widths, time delay (mu), and frequency offsets.
main_assumptions: Rotating-wave approximation, Wigner-Weisskopf approximation, unidirectional field, and an initial ground state population.
figures_summary: Figures show the excitation probability and marginal distributions as functions of time and decay rate ratios; plots of optimized parameters (widths, delays, detunings) for various light states; and a comparison of excitation efficiency for coherent vs. two-photon states.
paper_structure: The paper begins with the analytical derivation of the optimal two-photon state, followed by an investigation of the spectral and temporal properties of this state. It then compares the ideal case to realistic Gaussian and coherent pulse models, analyzing the impact of decay rate ratios and detuning, and concludes with an appendix detailing the dynamical equations and optimized parameter landscapes.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for quantum optics and open quantum systems as it provides fundamental guidance for designing efficient light-matter interfaces and explores how photon indistinguishability and quantum interference can be leveraged to control nonlinear atomic excitation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Joint spectral and temporal distributions together with the marginal distributions for the optimal two-photon state |2opt⟩obtained for the excitation of an atom with the following parameters: δa = 2.5 Γf and the ratios: Γe/Γf = 0.1 (plots (a) and (d)), Γe/Γf = 1 (plots (b) and (e)), and Γe/Γf = 10 (plots (c) and (f)).

FIG. 2: Maximum excitation probability of state |f⟩, P max f , as a function of the ratio Γe/Γf for the atom with δa = 0 and the input two-photon light defined by (29) with fixed δf = 0 satisfying the resonance condition. Results for the optimized widths of Gaussian profiles with and without optimized value of µ.

FIG. 3: Maximum excitation probability of state |f⟩, P max f , as a function of the ratio Γe/Γf and |δa|/Γf for the input two-photon light defined by (29). (a) Results for δf = 0 (ωa = ωb = ω0) and ω0 = ωfg/2 with the optimized width of Gaussian profiles and µ. (b) Results for ωa + ωb = ωfg with the optimized widths of Gaussian profiles and the values of µ and δf.

FIG. 4: Joint spectral and temporal distributions together with the marginal distributions for the two-photon state defined by (29) obtained for optimal excitation of an atom with the following parameters: δa = 2.5 Γf and the ratios: Γe/Γf = 0.1 (plots (a) and (d)), Γe/Γf = 1 (plots (b) and (e)), and Γe/Γf = 10 (plots (c) and (f)). The corresponding optimized parameters are: for Γe/Γf = 0.1, Ωa = 0.15 Γf , Ωb = 1.50 Γf , µΓf = 9.99 , and δf = 2.49 Γf ; for Γe/Γf = 1, Ωa = 1.02 Γf , Ωb = 1.78 Γf , µΓf = 1.05 , and δf = 2.09 Γf ; and for Γe/Γf = 10, Ωa = 2.71 Γf , Ωb = 2.79 Γf , µΓf = 0.04 , and δf = 4.16 Γf.

FIG. 6: Maximum excitation probability P max f , as a function of the ratio Γe/Γf and δa/Γf for the input two-photon light defined by (39). (a) Results for δf = 0 (ω01 = ω02 = ω0) and ω0 = ωfg/2 with the optimized widths Ω±. (b) Results for ω01 + ω02 = ωfg with the optimized widths Ω± and δf.

FIG. 5: Maximum excitation probability of state |f⟩, P max f , as a function of the ratio Γe/Γf for the atom with δa = 0 and for the input two-photon light defined by (39) with fixed δf = 0 satisfying the resonance condition. Results for the optimized width Ω±, with and without optimized value of µ.

FIG. 7: Joint spectral and temporal distributions together with the marginal distributions for the two-photon state defined by (39) obtained for optimal excitation of an atom with the following parameters: δa = 2.5 Γf and the ratios: Γe/Γf = 0.1 (plots (a) and (d)), Γe/Γf = 1 (plots (b) and (e)), and Γe/Γf = 10 (plots (c) and (f)). The corresponding optimized parameters are: for Γe/Γf = 0.1, Ω+ = 0.42 Γf , Ω−= 0.28 Γf , and δf = 2.45 Γf ; for Γe/Γf = 1, Ω+ = 0.89 Γf , Ω−= 1.21 Γf , and δf = 1.81 Γf ; and for Γe/Γf = 10, Ω+ = 1.03 Γf , Ω−= 11.09 Γf , and δf = 0.002 Γf.

Figure 10 shows the excitation of state |f⟩and the squared modulus of the normalized amplitude as a func- tion of time for three different ratios Γe/Γf = 0.1, 1, and 10, with δa = 0 and a field central frequency res- onant with the atomic transition frequencies, i.e., ω0 = ωeg = ωfe. In each case, the maximum excitation prob- abilities are lower than the maximum values obtained by optimizing the parameters of two-photon states. We have checked moreover that while an increase in the av- erage photon number enhances the excitation efficiency, the process remains most effective for the coherent pulse for atoms with comparable values of the constants Γe and Γf.

FIG. 8: Marginal distribution p(t) (upper panels) and excitation probability from the ground state to the final state, Pf(t) (lower panels), as functions of the scaled time tΓf for three values of the ratio Γe/Γf = 0.1, 1, and 10 in the panels (a), (b), and (c). The blue dashed line corresponds to the optimal state, the green dash-dotted line - to the state given by Eq. 29, and the orange dotted line - to the state given by Eq. 39. The parameters used in each case are the same as those presented in Figures 1, 4, and 7, respectively.

FIG. 9: Maximum excitation probability of the state |f⟩, ρmax ff , as a function of the ratio Γe/Γf and δa/Γf for a coherent Gaussian pulse with mean photon number ¯n = 2 satisfying the double-resonance condition, i.e., ω0 = ωfg/2. Results for the optimized width of Gaussian profile.
